skill.name: pufferlib
skill.description: Guides PufferLib reinforcement learning workflows, including PPO training, custom PufferEnv development, vectorized simulation, policies, and framework integration.
skill.summary: Build and optimize high-performance reinforcement learning workflows with PufferLib.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, weak crypto, sensitive file, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found these are mostly markdown code examples, RL terminology, environment variable names, or diagnostics, not hidden execution paths. The remaining concern is legitimate but user-visible: optional WandB or Neptune logging can transmit experiment configuration, and the training template writes checkpoints to a user-selected path.

medium_findings.title: Optional Third-Party Experiment Logging
medium_findings.description: The training template can initialize WandB or Neptune loggers and pass experiment configuration or a Neptune token when the user enables those modes. This is legitimate RL tooling, but it can transmit metadata to external services and should be disclosed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The logger calls and token argument are explicit in the training template and documentation. Risk is moderate because logging is optional and user configured, but external transmission is plausible when enabled.

low_findings.title: User-Selected Checkpoint File Writes
low_findings.description: The training template creates a checkpoint directory and writes model checkpoint files. This is expected for RL training, but users should choose a safe project directory and avoid overwriting important paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are clearly present and controlled by CLI arguments. The behavior is normal for training workflows and does not indicate malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Are Not Hidden Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings point to fenced documentation examples such as puffer train, torchrun, and installation commands. They are user-visible instructions and are not executed automatically by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown code fences or installation instructions. There is no evidence that these commands run during skill activation.

low_findings.title: Diagnostics and RL Terms Triggered Scanner Noise
low_findings.description: Weak crypto and reconnaissance detections map to RL concepts, validation assertions, GPU diagnostics, or environment variable names. No evidence found of cryptographic code, credential harvesting, or system reconnaissance intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context shows ordinary PPO parameters, validation checks, and GPU selection output. These do not support the scanner labels as real security issues.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Logger Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The template supports logger backends that may send training configuration and metrics to third-party services when selected. Users should avoid placing secrets in experiment configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicit and common for ML training. It is not malicious, but it is a real data-sharing surface.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Checkpoint Path Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The template writes model checkpoints under a CLI-selected directory. This is normal for training, but the path should be reviewed before running generated scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Checkpoint writes are directly implemented and are expected behavior. The risk depends on where the user points the checkpoint directory.

content.value_statement: Reinforcement learning projects need fast training, reliable environments, and clear integration paths. This skill guides PufferLib training, vectorization, policies, and environment setup.
actual_capabilities: Explains PufferLib PPO training workflows with CLI and Python patterns.
Provides templates for single-agent and multi-agent PufferEnv development.
Guides vectorized simulation choices, including worker counts and batch sizing.
Covers PyTorch policy patterns for MLP, CNN, LSTM, and multi-agent use cases.
Documents integration with Gymnasium, PettingZoo, Atari, Procgen, and related RL environments.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for performance, logging, checkpoints, and environment compatibility.
limitations: It does not install PufferLib or verify the local CUDA and PyTorch setup.
Training examples may need adaptation for current PufferLib APIs and project dependencies.
External logging guidance can involve third-party services when users enable those loggers.
It provides templates and guidance, not guaranteed benchmark performance on every machine.

use_cases.title: Train an RL Baseline
use_cases.description: Use the training template and guidance to configure PPO experiments on PufferLib or compatible environments.

use_cases.title: Build a Custom Environment
use_cases.description: Start from the PufferEnv templates to define observations, actions, rewards, resets, and multi-agent behavior.

use_cases.title: Optimize Simulation Throughput
use_cases.description: Apply vectorization guidance to tune workers, environments per worker, batch sizes, and shared-memory patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Start a PufferLib Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up a basic PufferLib training project for a Gymnasium environment. Explain the files I need and the first run command.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Custom Environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PufferEnv for my task with vector observations, discrete actions, reward shaping, reset logic, and a small validation checklist.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Training Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my PufferLib training configuration and suggest changes for num_envs, num_workers, batch size, device use, logging, and checkpointing.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Agent Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a multi-agent PufferLib pipeline with environment structure, shared or independent policies, vectorization strategy, metrics, and failure checks.

output_examples.input: I want to train Procgen CoinRun with PufferLib on one GPU.
output_examples.output: A concise training plan with recommended environment count, PPO settings, logging choice, checkpoint cadence, and validation steps before long runs.

output_examples.input: I need a custom grid-world environment for four cooperative agents.
output_examples.output: A structured environment design covering per-agent observations, shared rewards, done conditions, action validation, reset behavior, and test scenarios.

output_examples.input: My PufferLib run is slower than expected.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist for worker count, batch size, CPU and GPU utilization, environment step cost, memory pressure, and profiling tools.

best_practices: Start with a known Gymnasium or Ocean environment before writing a custom environment.
Validate reset, step, observation spaces, rewards, and done flags before scaling training.
Keep third-party logging disabled until you decide what configuration and metrics may be shared.
anti_patterns: Do not start large distributed runs before a short local smoke test passes.
Do not put API tokens or secrets into experiment configuration sent to loggers.
Do not optimize vectorization before measuring the environment step bottleneck.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps users build, train, integrate, and optimize reinforcement learning workflows with PufferLib.

faq.question: Does it include runnable templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Python templates for training and custom environment development.

faq.question: Can it help with multi-agent RL?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers multi-agent environments, shared policies, independent policies, and PettingZoo integration.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: Not by itself. Generated training scripts can use WandB or Neptune if the user enables those loggers.

faq.question: Is CUDA required?
faq.answer: No. The templates support CPU use, but CUDA is recommended for high-throughput training.

faq.question: Can it guarantee millions of steps per second?
faq.answer: No. Throughput depends on the environment, hardware, vectorization settings, and policy cost.
